Notice Of Motion And Motion To Conduct Punitive Damages Discovery
Off Calendar Aug-25-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JAMES KAWAHITO, Esq. of Kawahito Law Group APC (Tel: 310-746-5300) for plaintiffs Maurice Smith and Jamielee Smith;
Atty. ROGELIO SERRANO, Esq. of Kaufman Dolowich Voluck (Tel: 415-926-7600) for defendant/cross-complainant/cross-defendant St. Francis Homes Association;
Atty. JESSICA A. NUDELMAN, Esq. of Law Offices of John A. Hauser (Tel: 714-571-0407) for defendant Northern Trees Tree Service.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS MAURICE SMITH and JAMIELEE SMITH's Motion To Conduct Punitive Damages Discovery.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs' motion to conduct punitive damages discovery is denied. Civil Code section 3295, subdivision (c) prohibits pretrial discovery with respect to the defendant's financial condition and profits related to the alleged wrongful conduct without an order permitting such discovery. Such an order may be entered at any time "if the court finds, on the basis of the supporting and opposing affidavits presented, that the plaintiff has established that there is a substantial probability that the plaintiff will prevail on the claim pursuant to Section 3294." (Civ. Code sec. 3295, subd. (c).) "[B]efore a trial court may enter an order allowing discovery of financial condition information under Civil Code section 3295, subdivision (c) . . . , it must (1) weigh the evidence presented by both sides, and (2) make a finding that it is very likely the plaintiff will prevail on his claim for punitive damages." (Jabro v. Superior Court (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 754, 755.) A prima facie showing sufficient to avoid summary judgment on the plaintiff's claim for punitive damages is insufficient. (Id. at 758.) Here, based on the parties' conflicting showings, the Court is unable to determine that this standard is met at this time.

Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Complaint To Add Request For Punitive Damages
Off Calendar Aug-25-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. JAMES KAWAHITO, Esq. of Kawahito Law Group APC (Tel: 310-746-5300) for plaintiffs Maurice Smith and Jamielee Smith;
Atty. ROGELIO SERRANO, Esq. of Kaufman Dolowich Voluck (Tel: 415-926-7600) for defendant/cross-complainant/cross-defendant St. Francis Homes Association;
Atty. JESSICA A. NUDELMAN, Esq. of Law Offices of John A. Hauser (Tel: 714-571-0407) for defendant Northern Trees Tree Service.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS MAURICE SMITH and JAMIELEE SMITH's Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Complaint To Add Request For Punitive Damages.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiffs' motion for leave to file a second amended complaint to add request for punitive damages is granted. Defendant St. Francis Homes Association's and Northern Trees Tree Service's evidentiary objections are overruled.

Plaintiffs present a sufficient prima facie showing of facts to allow them to present their request for punitive damages to the trier of fact. (See Looney v. Superior Court (1993) 16 Cal.App.4th 521, 539 ["The trial court is not required to make any factual determination or to become involved in any weighing process beyond that necessarily involved in deciding whether a prima facie case for punitive damages exists. Once the court concludes that such a case can be presented at trial it must permit the proposed amended pleading to be filed. If it concludes that no such case exists, then it properly rejects the proposed pleading amendment. In making this judgment, the trial court's consideration of the defendant's opposing affidavits does not permit a weighing of them against the plaintiff's supporting evidence, but only a determination that they do not, as a matter of law, defeat that evidence."].) The trial court "must evaluate the evidence presented with [the clear and convincing] evidentiary burden in mind. (Id. at 539-540.)

Here, plaintiffs show that defendants were aware of at least two prior incidents of limbs falling from trees, that defendant St. Francis Homes Association had declined to follow its landscape management consultant's advice to do a risk assessment of the trees despite an insurance representative's warning of the very risk that occurred here-a limb falling on a person's head, causing serious personal injury-and that defendants failed timely to remove the dead limb that caused the incident. (Kawahito Decl., Exs. 1-4, Ex. 5 ["Consider a wors[t] case scenario, I am sure the HOA would not like to be in the middle of a limb knocking a person in the head"]; Ex. 6 (Hill Depo. at 104, 124-12).) Such evidence is sufficient to make a prima facie showing that defendants engaged in conduct showing a conscious disregard for public safety. It will be up to the trier of fact to determine whether the evidence is sufficient to show malice or oppression under the clear and convincing evidence standard.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)

DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-06-2021 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT STEVE ORMAND's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant Ormond's demurrer to plaintiffs' first amended complaint is overruled in its entirety. Defendant's request for judicial notice is denied. "[A] court cannot by means of judicial notice convert a demurrer into an incomplete evidentiary hearing in which the demurring party can present documentary evidence and the opposing party is bound by what that evidence appears to show." (Fremont Indemnity Co. v. Fremont General Corp. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 97, 115.)

Defendant argues he owed no duty towards plaintiff Maurice Smith. However, liberally construing the complaint, plaintiffs sufficiently plead duty as an element of a negligence cause of action. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 452; see Civ. Code, sec. 1714, subd. (a) [all persons are required to use due care in managing their activities and property].) The first amended complaint alleges defendant St. Francis Homes Association engaged defendant Ormond to "maintain the street trees in the common areas of St. Francis" and to "provide management and consulting services related to landscaping and tree maintenance services." (FAC pars. 4, 20; see also id. par. 30 [alleging that property was jointly maintained by all defendants including Ormond].) On March 18, 2018, acting as St. Francis's agent, defendant Ormond sent an email to Northern Trees asking them to remove the "low dead branch over the street" that three weeks later fell and injured plaintiff. (Id. pars. 8, 32 & 36.) Defendant Ormond owed a duty to plaintiff to keep the tree in a safe condition and was negligent in performing his tree maintenance services. (Id. pars. 45, 51 & 54.) The scope of defendant Ormond's actual contractual duties and his relationship with St. Francis raise factual issues that cannot be decided on the pleadings. "Duty, being a question of law, is particularly amenable to resolution by summary judgment." (Parsons v. Crown Disposal Co. (1997) 15 Cal.4th 456, 465; see also, e.g., Formet v. The Lloyd Termite Control Co. (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 595 [whether defendant pest inspection company owed legal duty to plaintiff to discover and disclose dry rot damage decided on summary judgment].) Whether defendant breached his duty of care similarly raises factual issues that cannot be resolved on the pleadings. At the demurrer stage, plaintiffs properly allege the elements for each cause of action in their first amended complaint.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)
